brand.cta: Follow for the next life recovered from silence.

sources.citation: Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History VII.15 (Hypatia, philosopher of Alexandria)
Damascius, Life of Isidore (fragmentary notice of Hypatia)
Watts, Edward J. — Hypatia: The Life and Legend of an Ancient Philosopher (Oxford, 2017)

beats.speech_text: Tonight: Hypatia of Alexandria — mathematician, philosopher, and the most famous woman of late antiquity.
She taught Neoplatonism in Alexandria, edited Ptolemy, and advised the city's prefect — a public intellectual in a city tearing itself apart.
Her world was Roman Egypt in the early fifth century: pagan temples closed, Christian factions armed, and the Library's tradition living on in private lecture halls.
Ἡ θεία γνῶσις φωτίζει τὸν νοῦν — divine knowledge illuminates the mind.
She became a symbol — of learning, of tolerance, and of what a city loses when debate turns to violence. Her story outlived the empire that killed her.
